data.title: Paramount considering taking Warner Bros. bid straight to holders - CNBC
data.description:
Paramount Skydance (PSKY) is evaluating taking an offer for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) straight to shareholders. 

The improved offer could be even higher than the $30/share all-cash offer it submitted this week, according to a CNBC report on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

A potential bid comes after Netlix (NFLX) earlier Friday agreed to buy Warner Bros Discovery (WBD), including its film and television studios, HBO Max and HBO, for $72 billion.

Shares of Warner Bros.(WBD) rose 5.3%, while Netflix (NFLX) fell 3.5% and Paramount SKydance dropped 6.5%.

data.title: MP Materials raised at Morgan Stanley on strategic importance to U.S. rare earth supply chain
data.description:
MP Materials (MP) +1.7% in Friday's trading as Morgan Stanley upgraded shares to Overweight from Equal Weight with a $71 price target, up from $68.50, saying the U.S. has effectively chosen MP as the U.S. solution to reshoring critical mineral supply chains, most recently highlighted by the company's deal with Ma'aden to develop a rare earth refinery in Saudi Arabia.

Morgan Stanley analyst Carlos De Alba said the deal provides significant optionality for MP (MP), as the Saudi refinery could ultimately provide another source of heavy rare earths for the company's magnet facilities, or if MP delivers on its upstream initiatives any excess mining output could be processed at the refinery.

China's one-year pause on rare earth restrictions does not solve dependency issues around critical minerals, De Alba wrote, noting permanent magnets remain under export restrictions and the export license process can be quite lengthy, but he believes MP (MP) is well positioned to benefit as the U.S. looks to develop a robust ex-China supply chain with MP at the center.

Project execution is the biggest risk now, but De Alba said MP (MP) has been delivering on its operating targets and has a strong balance sheet to support the downstream transition; if successful, the analyst believes recycling and the newly announced JV with Ma'aden have the
potential to significantly boost MP's ability to expand its magnetics business.

data.title: U.S. stocks in the green as NFLX-WBD acquisition fuels rally, investors assess inflation gauge
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were higher on Friday, as traders assessed fresh inflation data in hopes of gauging the Federal Reserve's monetary policy, while news of Netflix ((NFLX)) acquiring Warner Bros. Discovery ((WBD)) dominated news.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.3% in afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.4%, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.4%. 

Streaming giant Netflix (NFLX) -3.3% announced it will acquire Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) +4.9%, including its film and television studios, HBO Max and HBO, for a total enterprise value of ~$82.7 billion.

“This creates basically a two-horse show. Disney (DIS), with physical assets and parks, similar to Comcast (CMCSA); and Netflix (NFLX) with tons of streaming and movie assets. Paramount (PSKY) [is] stuck now treading water. [David Ellison, CEO of Paramount Skydance] is the big loser here. Cable assets will all be shed from all. Disney needs to do the same next,” said Ross Gerber, co-founder, CEO and CIO of Gerber Kawasaki.

In economic news, core PCE inflation stalled at +0.2% MoM in September as expected. Meanwhile, December consumer sentiment was higher at 53.3 vs. 52.0 consensus and 51.0 in November, according to the initial UofM Survey.

“Income [is] up 0.4%, spending 0.3%. However, inflation adjusted spending was flat which implied a soft spending path heading into the holiday spending season,” said Joseph Brusuelas, RSM U.S. principal and chief economist.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 4 basis points to 4.14%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 4 basis points to 3.57%.

data.title: Halliburton extends winning streak to eight sessions
data.description:
Oilfield services company Halliburton (HAL) rose 2.44% to $28.51 in the afternoon trade on Friday marking an eight consecutive session of gain.

The strong run comes amid oil prices being lifted by a mix of geopolitical tensions, stalled peace negotiations and a tightening supply outlook. Ukranian drone attacks on tankers and Russia’s Novorossiysk export terminal have added to those concerns, while the U.S. has tightened pressure on Venezuela by closing its airspace.

At the same time OPEC+ has kept productions steady, and expectations of a U.S. interest-rate cut have lifted hopes for stronger energy demand. Together, these factors have acted as tailwinds for rising crude benchmarks in the recent weeks.

However, Seeking Alpha analyst Richard Durant said Halliburton is acting as a “macro barometer” for the industry, noting that North American demand weakness and cautious customer spending are weighing on the outlook. He added that while cost-cutting has helped, “a genuine downturn would likely see operating margins fall into the mid- to low single-digit range,” and investors may be “overly optimistic towards the company's near-term prospects” amid broader macro risks.

According to Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system, Halliburton has a Hold rating with a score of 3.31 out of 5, supported by B in profitability, B- in momentum, and A- in revisions. However, weaker grades of D in valuation and D- in growth have weighed down the overall rating.

SA analysts also maintain a Hold rating on HAL.

Wall Street, however, is more optimistic with 18 out of 26 analysts rate the stock Buy or higher, while eight recommend holding. 

Shares have gained 3.98% over the past month and are up 3.73% year-to-date.

data.title: UBS stock gains after report Swiss set to scale back part of capital hit
data.description:
UBS Group (UBS) shares advanced 5% in Friday afternoon trading in the U.S. after a media report that the Swiss government is preparing to potentially scale back parts of a banking rule package that would force the lender to add over $20B in capital.

The possible reduction in UBS's (UBS) capital burden is tied to planned adjustments for the valuation of deferred assets and software, Reuters reported, citing two sources familiar with the matter.

It was reported in September that UBS (UBS) and the Swiss government were privately moving toward a compromise on tough new capital rules, potentially revising down the proposed ~$25B capital hike. 

UBS (UBS) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

UBS shares were on track to close out the week with a 5.7% gain, bringing its year-to-date increase to 34%.

data.title: REITs see a rise in valuation in November - report
data.description:
Equity REITs saw a rise in their average price-to-net asset value valuations in November after two consecutive months of decline, data from S&P Global Market Intelligence showed.

Median discount to the consensus NAV per share estimate fell by 3.1 percentage points month-over-month to 18.2%, the December 4 report said.

The analysis takes into account equity REITs that trade on the Nasdaq, NYSE, or NYSE American with a market capitalization of at least $200M.

Listed below are the REITs that closed November at the largest discount to NAV estimates:

  • Service Properties Trust (SVC) - $1.74 closing price represents a 63.4% discount to $4.75 NAV consensus
  • Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN) - $3.43 closing price represents a 57.5% discount to $8.07 NAV consensus
  • Industrial Logistics Properties Trust (ILPT) - $5.54 closing price represents a 51.7% discount to $11.47 NAV consensus
  • Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) - $53.67 closing price represents a 48.5% discount to $104.20 NAV consensus
  • Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH) - $6.57 closing price represents a 48.2% discount to $12.68 NAV consensus
  • Americold Realty Trust (COLD) - $10.83 closing price represents a 47.4% discount to $20.59 NAV consensus

Meanwhile, below REITs closed November at the largest premium to NAV estimates:

  • Welltower (WELL) - $208.22 closing price represents a 126.3% premium to $92.00 NAV consensus
  • American Healthcare REIT (AHR) - $50.78 closing price represents a 87.6% premium to $27.07 NAV consensus
  • Medical Properties Trust (MPW) - $5.76 closing price represents a 77.3% premium to $3.25 NAV consensus
  • Crown Castle (CCI) - $91.28 closing price represents a 57.9% premium to $57.80 NAV consensus
  • CareTrust REIT (CTRE) - $37.53 closing price represents a 56.6% premium to $23.97 NAV consensus
  • National Health Investors (NHI) - $79.48 closing price represents a 49.3% premium to $53.24 NAV consensus

data.title: Barclays sees brighter 2026 for multi-industry stocks but downgrades Pentair
data.description:
Barclays analysts struck a cautiously optimistic tone on the U.S. multi-industry sector for 2026, forecasting a modest rebound in organic growth and earnings after a lackluster 2025, even as they maintained a Neutral industry view.

In a broad outlook published Dec. 4, the team led by Julian Mitchell said subdued investor expectations outside of datacenters and electric utilities could set the stage for stronger relative performance next year, helped by improving volume growth and fading tariff-related uncertainty.

The analysts downgraded Pentair (PNR) to Equal Weight from Overweight, lowering their price target to $115 from $127, arguing that recent margin gains are likely “near their peak” and that future upside will depend more heavily on top-line growth as restructuring benefits fade. They also raised estimates and targets for several electrical-equipment names -- GE Vernova (GEV), nVent (NVT) and Vertiv (VRT) -- citing robust datacenter demand, particularly for liquid-cooling technologies still early in adoption.

Barclays noted that 2025 has closely resembled 2024 from a demand standpoint: strength in datacenters, electric utilities, and aerospace, but muted or declining activity elsewhere as both B2B and consumer spending remained soft.

Despite heavy market enthusiasm around AI, onshoring, and electrification, the median multi-industry stock has fallen about 11% relative to the S&P 500 this year, with earnings forecasts little changed and valuation multiples slightly compressed.

Looking to 2026, analysts expect industry organic sales to accelerate from roughly 3.5% this year to about 5%, driven by improving conditions in depressed categories such as residential HVAC, on-highway trucks, construction machinery, test and measurement, and factory automation.

Operating margins are projected to rise by around 70 basis points, similar to 2025, as cost inflation stabilizes and tariff effects normalize. EPS growth for the sector is expected to reach the low double-digits, roughly in line with the long-term average.

Barclays’ screening work highlights a group of names that screen especially well heading into 2026, including Carrier (CARR), GE Vernova (GEV), Lennox International (LII), nVent Electric (NVT), Ralliant (RAL) and Rockwell Automation (ROK). By contrast, stocks such as Allegion (ALLE), Eaton (ETN), Illinois Tool Works (ITW), Otis Worldwide (OTIS), Pentair (PNR) and Roper Technologies (ROP) appear less compelling based on the firm’s blend of fundamental trends and valuation factors.

data.title: Barclays explores bid for British weath manager Evelyn - report
data.description:
Barclays (BCS) is evaluating an offer to purchase Evelyn Partners, one of Britain's largest wealth managers.

Evelyn's private equity owners Permira and Warburg Pincus have asked potential buyers to submit offers on Dec. 10, according to a Reuters report on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter. The sale has also drawn interest from NatWest Group (NWG), Lloyds (LYG) and Royal Bank of Canada (RY).

Evelyn started a sales process earlier this year that could value the wealth manager at more than 2.5 billion pounds ($3.3 billion), Reuters reported in July. 

Barclays, NatWest, Lloyds, RBC, Permira and Warburg Pincus declined to comment to Reuters,

data.title: Market Voices: Warren slams WBD-Netflix deal, US warns on NATO
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact markets, sectors, or individual stocks.

  • U.S. Senator Elizabeth Warren said that Netflix's (NFLX) proposed $72 billion takeover of Warner Bros. Discovery's (WBD) studio and streaming operations was an "anti-monopoly nightmare" that would end up hurting consumers. 

“This deal looks like an anti-monopoly nightmare. A Netflix-Warner Bros. would create one massive media giant with control of close to half of the streaming market—threatening to force Americans into higher subscription prices and fewer choices over what and how they watch, while putting American workers at risk," Warren said in a statement.

Under the deal, Netflix will acquire Warner Bros. studios, the HBO network, and their accompanying content libraries. WBD intends to proceed with previous plans to spin out its TV networks, including CNN and TNT, into a separate company. 

Netflix had been in a bidding war for WBD's assets with Paramount Skydance (PSKY) and Comcast (CMCSA).

  • The Pentagon has reportedly told European officials that it expects Europe to assume most of NATO's defense capabilities, including intelligence and missiles, by 2027.

U.S. officials also said that if European members are unable to meet the deadline, the "U.S. may stop participating in some NATO defense coordination mechanisms", according to Reuters, which cited sources with knowledge of the discussions. 

Reuters added that some European officials view the 2027 deadline as unrealistic. It noted that some of the capabilities the U.S. provides, such as intelligence and surveillance, cannot be easily replaced and that much of the military equipment needed would take years to manufacture.

data.title: ETFs with heavy Netflix and Warner Bros. allocations are set to ride the media shakeup
data.description:
Friday’s market spotlight has been on Netflix (NFLX), which announced plans to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD)—including its film and television studios—for an enterprise value of approximately $82.7 billion.

The blockbuster deal has captured widespread attention across the media and communication services sectors, highlighting the ongoing consolidation shaping the entertainment industry. Investors and fund managers are also watching closely, as both companies are widely held across exchange-traded funds.

Current data shows that roughly 432 million Netflix shares are spread across 483 ETFs, while Warner Bros. Discovery accounts for about 594 million shares in 313 funds. The concentration of holdings underscores the potential ripple effects this merger could have across diversified portfolios.

Outlined below are Wall Street’s top 10 ETFs that have the largest portfolio claims in in NFLX and WBD:

Top 10 ETFs with the Largest Exposure to NFLX

No. 1: T-Rex 2X Long NFLX Daily Target ETF (NFLU), 66.35% allocation.

No. 2: Roundhill NFLX WeeklyPay ETF (NFLW), 19.95% allocation. 

No. 3: Direxion Daily NFLX Bull 2X Shares (NEXL), 12.87% allocation.
